title: Dynamics in Dimension 3: Geometry of Birkhoff Sections
speaker: Etienne Ghys
abstract: Birkhoff sections have been invented... by Poincaré in his work on celestial mechanics. Birkhoff made an extensive use of this concept in dynamical systems. Sometimes, one can find surfaces transverse to the trajectories of a vector field in a 3-manifold. If this happens, the dynamics of the vector field is somehow reduced to the dynamics of the first return map on the surface. This technique is especially useful to describe the behavior of geodesics on surfaces.

proof_stages.label: From negative curvature to Anosov stability
proof_stages.summary: The talk begins from Hadamard-Morse coding of geodesics on negatively curved surfaces and Gromov's metric-independence theorem, then identifies Anosov's stable/unstable splitting as the mechanism behind structural stability.

proof_stages.label: Classical Anosov models and the classification crisis
proof_stages.summary: The speaker recalls the two classical model families, geodesic flows and suspensions of hyperbolic toral automorphisms, then explains how Thurston's many examples destroyed the naive hope of classifying Anosov flows up to ordinary conjugacy.

proof_stages.label: Birkhoff sections as reductions to surface maps
proof_stages.summary: Birkhoff's construction is used to show how a three-dimensional flow can be cut by a transverse surface bounded by periodic orbits, so that the flow is understood through a first-return map, in the genus-two case even by an explicit SL(2,Z) matrix.

proof_stages.label: Almost conjugacy and the weakened uniqueness conjecture
proof_stages.summary: The proposed classification replaces ordinary conjugacy by almost conjugacy, and then virtual almost conjugacy, where finitely many periodic orbits and finite covers are allowed to be removed; Dehornoy's theorem is stated as saying that all known Anosov flows satisfy this weakened equivalence.

proof_stages.label: Dehornoy's Birkhoff-section constructions
proof_stages.summary: The speaker sketches the geometric-topological-number-theoretic constructions behind Dehornoy's theorem, using triangle groups, explicit transverse tori, and return maps expressible as words in SL(2,Z).

proof_stages.label: Left-handed flows and sections from positive linking
proof_stages.summary: A second mechanism for producing Birkhoff sections is introduced: a flow on a homology sphere is left-handed when invariant measures have positive linking, equivalently when a positive Gauss linking form exists, and then any finite collection of periodic orbits bounds a transverse section.

proof_stages.label: Arithmetic Fuchsian cases and limits of positivity
proof_stages.summary: The known left-handed examples are discussed, from the Hopf and modular flows to compact triangle-group quotients such as (2,3,7), while four-puncture examples show that the desired positivity phenomenon does not extend uniformly.

proof_stages.label: Generic existence of Birkhoff sections
proof_stages.summary: The talk closes its mathematical program with the conjectural question whether having at least one Birkhoff section is C^1-generic among nonsingular vector fields on a three-manifold.

examples.name: Hadamard pair-of-pants coding
examples.note: A negatively curved pair of pants cut by three arcs gives bi-infinite words in a,b,c with no repeated adjacent letter, making concrete how geodesic dynamics can be symbolically encoded.
examples.engagement_reason: He cuts the pair of pants by three intervals, codes a geodesic by the resulting letter sequence, and explains the no-repeat rule and uniqueness theorem for that coding.

passes.reason: The three-holed pair of pants is the minimal surface-building-block used positively to illustrate Hadamard symbolic coding.

the pair of pants is the minimal base surface for negative-curvature surface dynamics, used as a positive coding model.
the thrice-holed pair of pants is the minimal hyperbolic-surface base case for Hadamard’s coding, used as an illustrative model rather than a refutation
the pair of pants is the minimal hyperbolic-surface building block, used here illustratively for symbolic coding rather than to refute a claim.

It is used as a positive symbolic-coding model, and the pair of pants is the minimal hyperbolic surface building block.
Hadamard’s pair of pants is a lowest-complexity hyperbolic surface/base case for symbolic geodesic coding, used illustratively rather than to refute a claim.

A negatively curved pair of pants is the minimal surface building block used to illustrate Hadamard symbolic coding.

examples.name: Hyperbolic toral suspensions with different quadratic fields
examples.note: Comparing two trace-greater-than-two matrices in SL(2,Z) shows that finite covers alone preserve arithmetic data, motivating the deletion of periodic orbits in virtual almost conjugacy.
examples.engagement_reason: He constructs the toral suspension from a hyperbolic matrix, traces the return action, and analyzes why finite covers preserve the associated quadratic field so periodic orbits must be deleted.

passes.reason: These are standard toral-suspension Anosov flows, but different quadratic fields are used to obstruct finite-cover conjugacy without deleting orbits.
toral Anosov suspensions from different quadratic fields are ordinary members of the suspension family but obstruct conjugacy by finite covers alone
Ordinary hyperbolic toral suspensions with different eigenvalue quadratic fields are used to obstruct virtual conjugacy by finite covers alone.
Ordinary hyperbolic toral suspensions with distinct eigenvalue fields obstruct the stronger idea that finite covers alone make them conjugate.
ordinary hyperbolic torus-suspension flows with different eigenvalue fields obstruct conjugacy by finite covers alone.
suspensions from hyperbolic SL(2,Z) matrices in different quadratic fields are used to obstruct conjugacy after finite covers alone

suspensions with different eigenvalue quadratic fields are used to show finite covers alone cannot give conjugacy, while the suspensions themselves are ordinary members of the standard toral Anosov family.
Different quadratic eigenvalue fields obstruct finite-cover conjugacy without deleting orbits, while the suspensions themselves are ordinary toral Anosov examples.
Different quadratic fields obstruct the naive claim that finite covers alone make toral suspensions conjugate.
Toral suspensions from hyperbolic SL(2,Z) matrices with different quadratic fields are ordinary family members but obstruct conjugacy by finite covers alone.
different quadratic fields obstruct virtual conjugacy by finite covers alone, showing periodic-orbit deletion is really needed
Different quadratic fields obstruct conjugacy after finite covers alone, though the suspensions themselves are ordinary hyperbolic toral examples.

examples.name: (2,3,7) triangle orbifold and the Klein quartic
examples.note: The geodesic flow on the PSL(2,R) quotient associated with the (2,3,7) triangle group has a simple Birkhoff section whose return map is the matrix [[2,1],[1,1]], illustrating Dehornoy's constructions.
examples.engagement_reason: He builds an explicit Birkhoff section for the (2,3,7) orbifold by choosing a segment and unit vectors, then identifies it as a once-punctured torus with first return map given by the matrix [[2,1],[1,1]].

passes.reason: The (2,3,7) orbifold/Klein quartic is a Hurwitz maximal-symmetry, minimal-area triangle example used as a positive Birkhoff-section case.
the (2,3,7) triangle/Klein quartic case is the Hurwitz maximal-symmetry compact triangle example used as a positive model
The (2,3,7) Hurwitz triangle case gives the Klein quartic with maximal 168 automorphisms and is used constructively.

the (2,3,7) orbifold/Klein quartic is Hurwitz-maximal and highly symmetric, used constructively for Birkhoff sections.
the (2,3,7) Hurwitz/Klein-quartic case has maximal 168-fold symmetry and minimal compact triangle-orbifold area, and is used positively
the (2,3,7) orbifold/Klein quartic is used as a beautiful positive example, and is extremal and highly regular through minimal hyperbolic orbifold area and the Hurwitz 168 automorphisms.
The Klein quartic/(2,3,7) case realizes the Hurwitz maximal-symmetry compact triangle situation and is used as a positive Birkhoff-section example.
It is a positive Birkhoff-section example, and the Klein quartic is a maximally symmetric Hurwitz surface from the minimal-area (2,3,7) triangle orbifold.
The (2,3,7) triangle orbifold/Klein quartic is a maximally symmetric Hurwitz/minimal-area triangle case, used as a positive Birkhoff-section example.

The (2,3,7) triangle/Klein quartic case is the Hurwitz minimal-area/maximal-automorphism example used positively to build a Birkhoff section.

examples.name: Hopf fibration as the model left-handed flow
examples.note: The fact that any two Hopf fibers link positively is presented as the motivating model for defining left-handed flows via positivity of linking.
examples.engagement_reason: He cites the Hopf fibration as the basic left-handed example and states that its fibers have linking number one, without deriving or computing this on the example.

passes.reason: The Hopf fibration is the highly regular homogeneous circle fibration of S^3 used as the model left-handed flow.

examples.name: Modular flow on SL(2,R)/SL(2,Z)
examples.note: The modular geodesic flow, realized topologically on the complement of the trefoil knot in S^3, illustrates a nontrivial left-handed flow with arithmetic periodic orbits.
examples.engagement_reason: He applies left-handedness to the modular flow, using its realization as the three-sphere minus the trefoil to conclude that arbitrary collections of modular periodic orbits bound transverse sections.

passes.reason: The modular flow is the highly regular arithmetic (2,3,∞) triangle-orbifold flow, extremal among cusped orientable orbifold examples, used positively for left-handedness.

examples.name: Four-puncture mixed-linking obstruction
examples.note: Dehornoy's examples with four punctures include periodic-orbit pairs of both linking signs, forcing the speaker to abandon the hoped-for universal positive modular-form explanation.
examples.engagement_reason: He only reports Pierre's four-puncture counterexamples with both positive and negative linking, without exhibiting or working through the obstruction.

passes.reason: The four-puncture case is an ordinary low-complexity surface example used to exhibit mixed positive and negative linkings, disproving left-handedness there.
